title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
AI progress is bottlenecked by mapping architectures to GPU hardware.
Hand-written custom kernels often fail to meet theoretical performance thresholds.





Rapid emergence of new ML architectures (Transformers, SSMs, linear attention).
Kernel support lags; e.g., FlashAttention-2 took years to optimize for H100.





Tensor cores provide 15-16x the FLOPs of general-purpose compute.
High-performance frameworks must prioritize keeping tensor cores fully utilized.





Threads execute in groups of 32 (warps) on physical execution units.
Memory layouts map logical data to physical threads; poor layouts cause bank conflicts.





Warps are grouped into blocks, sharing fast shared memory (SMEM).
Higher occupancy (more warps per block) helps hide latencies but increases resource contention.





Grids launch multiple thread blocks across Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs).
Blocks communicate via slow HBM, with L2 cache helping to reduce latency.





Execution time is the max of memory, compute, and overhead costs.
Ideal performance requires perfect overlapping of memory loads, compute, and tensor core ops.





CUTLASS offers high performance but relies on myriad nested C++ templates.
Triton provides simpler interfaces but limits access to specialized hardware instructions.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS

Hand-written custom kernels often fail to meet theoretical performance thresholds.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
CUTLASS offers high performance but relies on myriad nested C++ templates.
Triton provides simpler interfaces but limits access to specialized hardware instructions.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
ThunderKittens Overview

A framework using a small, opinionated set of abstractions for high-performance AI kernels.
Maps directly to the three levels of the GPU hierarchy: warp, block, and grid.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Warp Parallelism: Tile Data Structures

Uses 16x16 matrix tiles as the basic data structure.
Maximizes compatibility with tensor cores and hardware-accelerated instructions.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Warp Parallelism: PyTorch-like Operations

Provides familiar parallel compute primitives (e.g., mma, exp, cumsum).
Enables static compile-time checking of layouts and operations to prevent bugs.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Managing Memory Layouts

Register tiles must match tensor core layouts; shared tiles need specific layouts for bulk copies.
TK automatically assigns shared tile layouts to minimize bank conflicts based on size and type.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Shared Memory Bank Conflicts

Naive row-major layouts suffer severe bank conflicts when loading into tensor cores.
TK uses swizzled layouts (32B, 64B, 128B) to eliminate conflicts while maintaining hardware support.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Block Parallelism: The LCSF Template

Generalized asynchronous template: Load, Compute, Store, Finish.
Reduces developer effort to populating boilerplate functions within a producer-consumer model.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Asynchronous Overlapping

Load/store workers manage HBM to SRAM movement using TMA.
Compute workers operate on fast memory, overlapping with asynchronous I/O.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Multi-stage Pipeline Buffers

Maintains N-stage pipelined buffers in shared memory.
Hides HBM load/store latency by allowing compute to proceed while next tiles load.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Occupancy vs. Efficiency Tradeoffs

Higher occupancy increases overlap but causes register contention.
TK parametrizes worker counts, letting developers easily tune the Pareto frontier.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Grid Parallelism: Persistent Block Launch

Launches thread blocks on all SMs upfront, reusing them for subsequent tasks.
Eliminates pipeline bubbles and setup/tear-down overheads.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
section: L2 Cache Reuse & Block Launch Order
text:
L2 Cache Reuse & Block Launch Order

Block launch order heavily influences L2 cache hit rates and HBM bandwidth.
TK provides optimized 3D strides to maximize data reuse across thread blocks.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Workhorse Kernels: GEMM

A single 40-line TK GEMM kernel matches CuBLAS and CUTLASS performance.
Avoids the 600MB+ complexity of highly tuned CuBLAS variants.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Workhorse Kernels: Attention

Matches FlashAttention-3 on non-causal forward passes.
Outperforms FA3 by 10-40% on causal and non-causal backward passes.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Emerging Architectures: Linear Attention

Outperforms Flash Linear Attention (FLA) by 14x on polynomial-based linear attention.
Achieves 6.5x speedup on learned feature map linear attention.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Emerging Architectures: State Space Models

Outperforms FlashFFTConv by up to 7.9x on long convolutions.
Beats Triton-based Mamba-2 kernels by over 3x through easy operation fusion.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Profiling: Long Convolution

NCU profiles show higher issue slot utilization and fewer memory stalls.
Tensor core utilization increases by 4.1x compared to FlashFFTConv.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Profiling: Attention Backwards

Matches FA3 in tensor core utilization but achieves higher issue slot utilization.
Incurs 85% fewer shared memory stalled cycles by eliminating bank conflicts.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Extensibility: FP8 & Padded Tiles

Supports FP8 precision GEMMs, matching CuBLAS performance.
Handles unaligned workloads via automatic padding without sacrificing performance.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
text:
Extensibility: Consumer & Personal Hardware

Competitive performance on NVIDIA RTX 4090 and Apple M2 Pro.
Demonstrates cross-platform portability with minimal code changes.

title: THUNDERKITTENS: SIMPLE, FAST, AND Adorable KERNELS
section: Simplicity: Library Size & Lines of Code
text:
Simplicity: Library Size & Lines of Code

TK library is under 1.0 MB, compared to 22 MB for CUTLASS.
TK kernels average <200 lines of code, significantly less than SOTA baselines.

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
text:
Transformer architecture dominates LLMs
Attention mechanism reads from KV-cache and computes outputs based on queries
Efficiency of attention operator is paramount to LLM inference performance





Various attention computation patterns

Prefill computation for context processing
Batched decoding during serving

Prefix-reuse opportunities across requests
Tree decoding in speculative scenarios
Query lengths and KV-cache sizes vary within batches and over time





Efficient memory layouts needed for growing KV-cache

Paged attention, radix trees

Hardware-specific pipelines required for each GPU architecture
Diverse attention mechanisms in modern LLMs (variants)

Grouped Query Attention (GQA)
Specialized masks, sliding window
Custom attention scores (logits soft-cap, FlashSigmoid)






Each system implements specialized attention solutions for specific subsets
Results in:

High maintenance overhead
Potential inefficiencies
Lack of flexibility for new attention variants

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
text:
Overview






Block-sparse format for unified KV-cache storage
Customizable attention template with JIT compilation
Dynamic load-balanced scheduling compatible with CUDAGraph

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
section: KV-Cache Storage: Block-Sparse Matrix as Unified Format
text:
KV-Cache Storage: Block-Sparse Matrix as Unified Format







Page Table represented in BSR format



Page tables, radix trees, and sparse masks unified under Block Sparse Row (BSR) format

Each block is either zeros or non-zeros.

Flexible block sizes (Br, Bc) allow fine-grained sparsity

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
text:
Two Meanings of “Block”

Block in Block-Sparse Matrix (BSR format)

Br (number of rows) is aligned with queries
Bc (number of cols) is aligned with tokens

Thread Block (CTA - Cooperative Thread Array)
Queries grouped in the same matrix block are processed by the same thread block.

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
text:
Composable Formats for Memory Efficiency







Composable formats for shared-prefix decomposition



Multiple block-sparse formats instead of single format
Shared prefixes: larger block sizes for shared memory access
Unique suffixes: smaller block sizes

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
section: Data Transfer: Global to Shared Memory
text:
Data Transfer: Global to Shared Memory






Tiles transferred from scattered global memory to contiguous shared memory
Asynchronous copy instructions (LDGSTS) with 128B width
Supports arbitrary block sizes not aligned with tensor core shapes

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
text:
Flexible Tile Size Selection

Tile sizes: (1, 16, 32, 64, 128) × (32, 64, 128)
Automatic selection heuristics:

Determine minimal query tile size based on average query length
Maximize SM resource occupancy given register/shared memory constraints

CUDA cores for tile size 1, tensor cores otherwise

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
text:
Load-Balanced Runtime Scheduler






Splits each query tile’s KV into chunks
Priority queue assigns chunks to CTAs balancing by cost
Cost function: \(cost(l_q, l_{kv}) = \alpha\cdot l_q  + \beta\cdot l_{kv}\)
Produces deterministic aggregation order

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
text:
CUDAGraph Compatibility

Persistent kernels with fixed grid size
Fixed workspace buffer offsets for consistent pointers
Attention and contraction stages merged into one persistent kernel
Plan phase runs on CPU, run phase captured in CUDAGraph

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
text:
Environment Setup

Hardware

NVIDIA A100 40GB SXM
NVIDIA H100 80GB SXM

Software

CUDA 12.4, PyTorch 2.4.0
FP16 precision for storage and computation

Baselines

SGLang with Triton backend
FlashAttention library

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
text:
End-to-End LLM Serving Performance






FlashInfer shows consistent ITL and TTFT improvements over Triton backend

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
text:
Input Dynamism






Batch size 16, three sequence length distributions

Constant (1024), Uniform (512 to 1024), Skewed (Zipf, avg 1024)

FlashInfer significantly outperforms FlashAttention on uniform and skewed distributions
Load-balanced scheduler addresses variable-length workloads

title: FlashInfer: Efficient and Customizable Attention Engine for LLM Inference Serving
text:
Customizability: Long-Context Inference






Streaming-LLM with fused RoPE + attention kernel
28-30% latency reduction across different settings
Fused kernel achieves 1.6-3.7× higher bandwidth utilization

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
LLMs use context-rich prefixes (history, documents) for better quality.
Prefixes significantly increase input length.
Problem: Longer inputs lead to high computation and Time To First Token (TTFT) latency.





Many requests share common prefixes.
Recomputing Key/Value (KV) tensors for prefixes is wasteful.
Solution: Store and reuse prefix KVs.
Existing systems cache KVs in GPU/CPU memory.




TTFT






GPU/CPU memory is limited.
Prefixes can be large (e.g., 3.4GB for OPT-30B, 2.6k tokens).
Offloading KVs to disk is necessary but slow.
Problem: Disk I/O latency can exceed recomputation time, negating reuse benefits.




TTFT






GPU/CPU memory is limited.
Prefixes can be large (e.g., 3.4GB for OPT-30B, 2.6k tokens).
Offloading KVs to disk is necessary but slow.
Problem: Disk I/O latency can exceed recomputation time, negating reuse benefits.




TTFT Breakdown






Research shows LLM inference quality is robust to discarding less important KVs.
Most impact comes from a subset of “important” tokens/KVs.
Opportunity: Can we leverage importance to optimize prefix KV reuse from slower storage?





Reduce the TTFT bottleneck caused by loading prefix KVs from disk.
Idea: Selectively load only the important prefix KVs.
Goal: Minimize I/O data transfer while maintaining inference quality.





Standard methods need all prefix KVs loaded into GPU to calculate attention scores.

Defeats the purpose of reducing I/O.

Importance is query-dependent: Statically pre-identifying important tokens for a prefix doesn’t work well (poor recall).




Left: Accuracy w.r.t KV recall ratio; Right: ORI is baseline, SPI means statical pre-identifying






Existing systems chunk KVs sequentially.

Loading important KVs forces loading unimportant ones in the same IO chunk (read amplification).

Standard caching (LRU/LFU) ignores KV importance.

Importance-rich chunks might be evicted prematurely.

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
IMPRESS Overview

An importance-informed, multi-tier (GPU, CPU, Disk) prefix KV storage system.
Key Components:

ITF: Important Token Identification
PKM: Importance-Informed Prefix KV Management (Reordering & Caching)

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
IMPRESS Dataflow

Request arrives (prefix + query).
Find longest reusable prefix (\(R\)) in radix tree.
ITF: Identify important KVs in \(R\) (\(R_{important}\)) with minimal I/O.
Load \(R_{important}\) (from disk/CPU if needed) to GPU.
Compute remaining prefill using \(R_{important}\) + non-reused prefix (\(NR\)) + query.
PKM: Store new KVs (\(NR\)) and manage caches/disk layout.

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
ITF Insight 1: Intra-Layer Similarity

Observation: The set of important token indices is highly similar across different attention heads within the same layer.
Intuition: Heads derive k/v from the same large K/V tensors.




Left: example of similarity between two heads (1/3); Right: Heatmap

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
ITF Insight 2: Similarity Across Scales

Observation: Similarity persists across different importance sampling ratios and LLM model sizes/depths.
Similarity tends to be lower for smaller ratios, smaller models, and deeper layers, but still significant.

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
Similarity-Guided Important Token Identification (ITF)

Idea: Use similarity to approximate global importance using only a few heads (“probe heads”).




Example: 4 tokens, 32 heads, use 3 probe heads

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
Similarity-Guided Important Token Identification (ITF)

Idea: Use similarity to approximate global importance using only a few heads (“probe heads”).




Reduced TTFT

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
Importance-Informed KV Management (PKM)

Addresses Challenge 2 (suboptimal storage/caching).
Two parts:

KV Reordering (Storage Layout)
Score-Based Cache Management

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
PKM: KV Reordering

Problem: Sequential KV layout leads to read amplification.
Solution: Periodically (offline) reorder tokens within prefix segments based on average importance.
Repack KVs: Group important KVs together into denser IO chunks.
Result: Reduces number of chunks read for important KVs.

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
PKM: Score-Based Cache Management

Problem: Standard LRU/LFU ignores KV importance, leading to poor GPU cache hit rates for important data.
Solution: Assign a score to each chunk:

Score = AccessFrequency * ImportanceRatio

ImportanceRatio = (% of important KVs in the chunk, updated dynamically).
Prioritize chunks with higher scores for GPU cache admission/retention.

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
Score-Based Caching: Dual-Cache Policy

Manages GPU and CPU caches (exclusive).
Uses two min-heaps (one per cache) storing chunk scores.
On access/load:

Update chunk score.
Promote chunk to GPU cache if score > lowest score in GPU heap.
Evict lowest-scored chunk from GPU/CPU heap if cache is full.

Ensures non-redundancy; replicas always exist on disk.

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
Setup

Models: OPT-{6.7B, 13B, 30B}, Llama2-{7B, 13B}
Hardware: AMD EPYC CPU, 128GB DRAM, NVIDIA A100 GPU (80GB), Intel NVMe SSD
Datasets: PIQA, RTE, COPA, OpenBookQA (Few-shot setup)
Baselines:

ReComp: No reuse.
AS-like: AttentionStore baseline (reuse all KVs, LRU GPU cache).
AS+H2O+LRU: AS-like + H2O importance (load important V only, LRU).
AS+H2O+LFU: AS-like + H2O importance (load important V only, LFU).

IMPRESS: Uses ITF + KV Reordering + Score-based Caching.

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
Model Generation Quality

Negligible accuracy drop (<0.2%)

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
Average TTFT Performance

1.2x - 2.8x TTFT reduction over the prior SOTA

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
I/O Time Reduction

1.5x - 3.8x reduction in I/O time for loading prefix KVs compared to prior SOTA

title: IMPRESS: An Importance-Informed Multi-Tier Prefix KV Storage System for Large Language Model Inference
text:
Breakdown



+RO: +KV Recorder; ALL: +Score-base caching

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
LLMs excel at tasks like dialogue, summarization, content creation.
Generation is typically autoregressive: predict next token based on previous ones.






KV Cache size scales linearly with sequence length and batch size.
Can exceed GPU memory, even more than model parameters.

Memory ∝ (batch × seq len); OPT-30B @ 128×1024 needs ≈ 180 GB KV alone

Major bottleneck for long content generation and high throughput deployment.





Simple cache size limits hurt accuracy significantly.
Existing sparse attention methods often require large caches or degrade pre-trained model performance.
Learned compression methods can be complex and add inference overhead.






LLM attention matrices are highly sparse (>95%) during inference.

only 5% of the KV cache is sufficient for decoding the same output token

Suggests not all past tokens are needed.






Accumulated attention scores follow a power-law distribution.
A small subset of tokens (“Heavy Hitters” - H₂) contributes most value.
H₂ correlate strongly with frequent co-occurring tokens.






Removing H₂ tokens drastically drops performance.
Retaining H₂ + recent tokens maintains accuracy effectively.






Calculating H₂ importance using only local statistics (past attention) is as effective as using global (future) information.
Makes a practical, low-cost eviction policy feasible.

only 5% of the KV cache is sufficient for decoding the same output token

Suggests not all past tokens are needed.

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
Accumulated attention scores follow a power-law distribution.
A small subset of tokens (“Heavy Hitters” - H₂) contributes most value.
H₂ correlate strongly with frequent co-occurring tokens.

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
Removing H₂ tokens drastically drops performance.
Retaining H₂ + recent tokens maintains accuracy effectively.

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
Calculating H₂ importance using only local statistics (past attention) is as effective as using global (future) information.
Makes a practical, low-cost eviction policy feasible.

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
H₂O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle

A novel, low-cost KV cache eviction policy.
Goal: Reduce memory footprint significantly while maintaining accuracy.

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
H₂O: Core Idea

Maintain a fixed-size KV cache by dynamically balancing:

Recent Tokens: Always keep the last N tokens (temporal locality).
Heavy Hitter (H₂) Tokens: Keep the M tokens with the highest accumulated attention scores.

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
H₂O: Eviction Policy


Score Calculation: At each step, update accumulated attention score for cached tokens based on current attention oᵢ.
Eviction: If cache is full, evict the token (excluding recent ones) with the lowest accumulated score.
New Token: Add the newly generated token to the recent set.

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
Implementation Details

Implemented on top of FlexGen inference engine.
Efficient management:

Pre-allocated memory buffers.
Circular queue for recent tokens.
Avoids expensive memory swaps or data movement.

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
Experimental Setup

Models: OPT (6.7B, 30B, 66B), LLaMA (7B, 13B, 30B), GPT-NeoX (20B).
Tasks: Summarization (XSUM, CNN/DM), QA (OpenBookQA), Reasoning (COPA, PiQA, RTE, WinoGrande), Math (MathQA). From HELM & lm-eval-harness.
Hardware: NVIDIA A100 80GB GPU.
Baselines: Full Cache, “Local” (only recent tokens), Sparse Transformers (Strided, Fixed).

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
Accuracy


H₂O (with 20% cache budget, i.e., 5x reduction) achieves comparable accuracy to the full cache model across tasks and models.
Significantly outperforms the “Local” baseline, which collapses at low budgets.
H₂O sometimes acts as a regularizer, slightly improving performance.

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
Throughput Improvements


H₂O enables much larger batch sizes or avoids CPU offloading due to reduced memory.
Up to 29x throughput improvement over DeepSpeed & Accelerate.
Up to 3x throughput improvement over FlexGen (state-of-the-art).

title: H2O: Heavy-Hitter Oracle for Efficient Generative Inference of Large Language Models
text:
Latency Reduction


With the same batch size, H₂O reduces generation latency.
Up to 1.9x lower latency compared to FlexGen for long sequences.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Deploying LLMs on edge devices (smartphones, robotics) enhances privacy and reduces latency.
Memory constraints necessitate low-bit weight quantization (4-bit, 2-bit, 1-bit).
Activation quantization lags behind due to outliers, leading to asymmetric precision (e.g., W4A16, W1A8).





Current hardware lacks native support for mixed-precision matrix multiplication (mpGEMM).
Existing systems resort to dequantizing low-bit weights to match high-precision activations.








General practice: Dequantize weights, then perform high-precision Matmul.


T-MAC: Bit-serial decomposition, precompute tables, and perform table lookup + sum.






Dequantization overhead offsets the gains from bit-width reduction.
Scaling down from 4-bit to 1-bit can actually increase latency in existing systems.
Fails to fully capitalize on the reduced memory bandwidth requirements of low-bit weights.





Different deployment scenarios require different bit-widths (1-bit to 4-bit).
Data layouts and kernels must be redesigned case-by-case for each mixed precision.
A unified, scalable solution independent of hardware data types is urgently needed.





Lookup Table (LUT) methods precompute products to avoid costly multiply-accumulate operations.
Explored on GPUs but suboptimal due to fixed architecture constraints (inadequate storage or slow access).
LUT-based mpGEMM on CPUs remains uncharted territory, offering a promising path for edge deployment.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Deploying LLMs on edge devices (smartphones, robotics) enhances privacy and reduces latency.
Memory constraints necessitate low-bit weight quantization (4-bit, 2-bit, 1-bit).
Activation quantization lags behind due to outliers, leading to asymmetric precision (e.g., W4A16, W1A8).

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Current hardware lacks native support for mixed-precision matrix multiplication (mpGEMM).
Existing systems resort to dequantizing low-bit weights to match high-precision activations.








General practice: Dequantize weights, then perform high-precision Matmul.


T-MAC: Bit-serial decomposition, precompute tables, and perform table lookup + sum.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Dequantization overhead offsets the gains from bit-width reduction.
Scaling down from 4-bit to 1-bit can actually increase latency in existing systems.
Fails to fully capitalize on the reduced memory bandwidth requirements of low-bit weights.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Different deployment scenarios require different bit-widths (1-bit to 4-bit).
Data layouts and kernels must be redesigned case-by-case for each mixed precision.
A unified, scalable solution independent of hardware data types is urgently needed.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Lookup Table (LUT) methods precompute products to avoid costly multiply-accumulate operations.
Explored on GPUs but suboptimal due to fixed architecture constraints (inadequate storage or slow access).
LUT-based mpGEMM on CPUs remains uncharted territory, offering a promising path for edge deployment.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
T-MAC Overview

T-MAC transforms data-type-centric multiplication to bit-wise table lookup.
Provides a unified and scalable mpGEMM solution for any mixed bit-width.







Offline: Bit-serial decomposition of weights, tiling, and permutation.
Online: Precompute LUT from activations, table look-up, and bit-serial aggregation.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Bit-Serial Decomposition

Multiplication is transformed into bit-wise calculation based on linear equivalent transformation.







Diverse weight layouts are reduced to a unified one-bit matrix layout.
Enables linear scale-down of computation cost with bit-width reduction.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
LUT-based mpGEMM Algorithm

A \(b\)-bit weight matrix is decomposed into \(b\) one-bit matrices.
For a group of \(g\) bits, there are only \(2^g\) possible permutations.
Activations are precomputed with all bit patterns and saved in a table.
mpGEMM is reduced to table lookup and addition, eliminating multiplications.







Left: T-MAC unpacks indices, looks up tables, splits/converts, and aggregates.
Right: General practice decodes weights and performs dot-product.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Challenges of LUT Implementation

Random Data Access: Tables are randomly accessed, requiring residence in fast on-chip memory (registers).
Enlarged On-Chip Memory: LUT size grows exponentially with group size \(g\), and vector outputs require more temporary storage.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
LUT-Centric Data Layout

Stores lookup tables in registers to accelerate random accesses.
Leverages hardware-specific instructions (e.g., TBL on ARM, PSHUF on x86).
Designs axis reordering and tiling to enhance data reuse and reduce memory pressure.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Axis Reordering & Tiling

Axis Reordering: Swaps loop order to temporal axis (\(K\)) first, maintaining a small lookup table \([1, 2^g]\) instead of a massive one.
Tiling: Carefully considers tile sizes \(N_{tn}, M_{tm}, K_{tk}\) to maximize lookup table reuse across the weight matrix.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Weight Permutation & Interleaving

Weight Permutation: Permutes weight matrix offline to align tile loads with sequential memory transactions, maximizing DRAM bandwidth.
Weight Interleaving: Reorders packed weights to eliminate backward reordering overhead during little-endian unpacking.







Interleaved weights can be unpacked sequentially using simple AND and SHR+AND operations.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Reducing LUT Storage: Mirror Consolidation

Exploits the symmetrical properties of table values (positive/negative pairs).
Only half of the table values need to be explicitly stored; the rest are reconstructed by negation.
Lossless compression that halves the table length and accelerates precomputation.







Mirror consolidation halves the table length; table quantization reduces the table width.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Reducing LUT Storage: Table Quantization

Quantizes table values (e.g., fp16 to int8) with a scaling factor to reduce table width.
Uses finer granularity and dynamic quantization to minimize accuracy degradation.
Combined with mirror consolidation, reduces storage footprint up to a quarter without accuracy loss.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Hardware Intrinsics for Fast Lookup

Utilizes 8-bit look-up instructions provided by ARM NEON and Intel AVX2.







NEON’s 128-bit width perfectly accommodates \(g=4\) tables.
AVX2 duplicates tables to fill 256-bit registers for parallel lookups.
Float16 tables are split into two 8-bit LUTs and recombined.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Fast 8-bit Aggregation

Aggregates look-up results in low bit (int8) before converting to higher precision (fp16).
Uses avg/rhadd instructions to compute averages and minimize overflow without int16 conversion.
Doubles throughput compared to int16 aggregation, offered as an optional optimization.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Bit-Serial Linear Transformation

Introduces a linear transformation \(f(v_i)\) to bit values to speed up precomputation and reduce quantization error.
Selects \(s_0 = -1\) and \(s_1 = 1\) to circumvent float-multiply instructions and minimize LUT entry differences.







Adjusts the decomposition of \(W\) to incorporate the transformed values and bias terms.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Experimental Setup

Devices: Apple M2-Ultra, Raspberry Pi 5, Jetson AGX Orin, Surface Book 3.
Models: Llama-2 (1/2/3/4-bit) and BitNet (1-bit, 1.58-bit).
Baselines: State-of-the-art llama.cpp (dequantization-based kernels).

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
mpGEMV Kernel Performance

llama.cpp fails to gain speedup as bits decrease, even slowing down at 3-bit due to decoding overhead.
T-MAC achieves linear speedup with bit reduction across all devices.







Single-threaded mpGEMV: Up to 11.2x speedup for 1-bit.
Multi-threaded mpGEMV: Constrained by memory bandwidth but still achieves significant speedup (e.g., 4.0x at 2-bit).

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
mpGEMM Kernel Performance

Evaluated with a sequence length of 256 for multi-threading.
T-MAC achieves maximum speedups of 4.0x, 5.3x, and 5.3x on RBP, Orin, and Surface for 2-bit.
M2-Ultra benefits from AMX coprocessor, but T-MAC still achieves 2.0x speedup for 1-bit.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
End-to-End Inference Throughput

Integrated T-MAC kernels into llama.cpp for real-world model evaluation.







Single-threaded: Up to 6.7x speedup on Raspberry Pi 5.
Multi-threaded: Peak of 71 tokens/sec on M2-Ultra and 11 tokens/sec on Raspberry Pi 5 for BitNet-3B.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Power and Energy Consumption

Measured on M2-Ultra using multi-threaded inference.







Power consumption reduced by 10.3% to 17.3% across models.
Total energy consumption reduced by 20.6% to 61.2% due to combined power and latency gains.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Optimization Breakdown

Progressive application of optimizations on M2-Ultra multi-threaded GEMV.







Table Quantization (TQ): Makes performance competitive with llama.cpp.
Tiling & Permutation: Yields up to 1.45x and 1.39x speedups respectively.
Interleaving: Eliminates unpacking overhead, achieving 1.42x speedup.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
Error Analysis

Two error sources: table quantization (algorithmic) and fast aggregation (instruction execution).







Table quantization introduces negligible NMSE difference compared to llama.cpp.
Fast aggregation increases NMSE by 2.5x but is optional for latency-critical, accuracy-tolerant scenarios.
End-to-end model quality (perplexity, accuracy) remains identical to llama.cpp without fast aggregation.

title: T-MAC: CPU Renaissance via Table Lookup for Low-Bit LLM Deployment on Edge
text:
CPU vs GPU Comparison

Compared T-MAC (CPU) with llama.cpp (GPU) on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin.







T-MAC significantly outperforms GPU on W1A16 and achieves comparable performance on W2A16/W3A16.
End-to-end: T-MAC (CPU) achieves 2.3x better energy efficiency than llama.cpp (GPU) despite slightly lower throughput.
